system_prompt: Summarize this GitHub issue or pull request thread. Return concise JSON only with keys problem_summary, solution_summary, maintainer_signal_summary, dedupe_summary. Each field should be plain text, no markdown, and usually 1-3 sentences.

results.title: Title channels.discord.proxy does not proxy REST API requests — guild resolve fails, guild channels broken behind HTTP proxy

summary.problem_summary: channels.discord.proxy only proxies the Discord WebSocket gateway; startup REST API calls (deploy commands, fetch bot identity) and the guild resolution path do not use the configured HTTP proxy, causing REST requests to time out and guild channels to be non-functional in environments that require a proxy.
summary.solution_summary: Apply the configured proxy to the undici HTTP dispatcher for all Discord REST calls (not just the gateway), ensure the guild-resolve code path uses the same proxied HTTP client and add retry logic; optionally add a global proxy config (e.g., gateway.proxy or network.proxy) to set a global ProxyAgent.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Issue labeled bug and regression; reporter provided logs, reproduction steps, and a partial workaround (global ProxyAgent) that fixes REST but not guild resolve; no maintainer comments or fixes in the thread yet.
summary.dedupe_summary: No duplicate referenced in the thread; this appears to be a regression distinct from WebSocket proxying because guild-resolve uses a different HTTP path — check other proxy-related issues but treat this as a new, actionable bug.

judge.reasoning: Identifies the core regression (REST/guild-resolve not proxied vs WebSocket) but includes meta/instructional noise ('No duplicate referenced', 'check other proxy-related issues') that reduces clarity and conciseness for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]:channels.discord.proxy doesn’t apply to outbound REST sends / Carbon RequestClient fetch has no proxy support

summary.problem_summary: Outbound Discord REST requests are not using the configured channels.discord.proxy, so sending messages/attachments fails with fetch failed in restricted networks while the WebSocket gateway (receiving) works via the proxy.
summary.solution_summary: Add proxy support to the Discord RequestClient/fetch implementation (or pass a proxy agent to undici/node fetch) so all REST calls and media uploads/downloads honor channels.discord.proxy; add tests and docs for proxy behavior.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: No maintainer responses in the thread; only a user bug report (labelled bug) with environment/version details and reproduction steps.
summary.dedupe_summary: Likely duplicates other requests for Discord REST/RequestClient proxy support or undici fetch proxying; search existing issues for "discord proxy", "RequestClient proxy", or "undici proxy" before filing a new one.

judge.reasoning: Mentions the relevant components (Discord REST/RequestClient and undici proxy support) but reads like a duplicate suggestion rather than a concise statement that outbound REST requests aren't using the configured proxy, so it has useful keywords but misses the core failure detail.

results.title: Discord can log in behind proxy, but native command deploy / bot identity fetch fail with 'fetch failed'

summary.problem_summary: When OpenClaw is configured to use an HTTP proxy for Discord, the provider can connect to the gateway and log in and can fetch the application ID, but some startup REST calls (deploying native commands and fetching bot identity) consistently fail with "fetch failed", leaving the Discord provider partially functional and triggering periodic stale-socket restarts.
summary.solution_summary: No confirmed fix in thread; suspected root cause is inconsistent proxy handling where some Discord REST requests bypass the proxy or use a different fetch/dispatcher path. Recommended action is to ensure all REST calls use the same proxy-aware fetch path; the reporter offered to provide more details or test a patch.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: No maintainer responses in the thread; the reporter supplied logs, environment details, reproduction notes, and offered to assist with testing.
summary.dedupe_summary: Not marked as a duplicate; distinct because the gateway/login succeeds while specific REST startup calls fail, indicating inconsistent proxy usage rather than token/config errors; should be compared to other proxy-related reports but appears unique.

judge.reasoning: The summary correctly highlights the core issue (gateway login succeeds while REST startup calls fail due to inconsistent proxy usage) but includes meta phrasing and omits the specific failing REST operations (native command deployment, bot identity fetch), so it's adequate for proxy-related clustering but could be more precise.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord proxy config recognized but REST API fails with fetch failed

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw reports Discord proxy as enabled but REST API calls fail with "fetch failed" under WSL2 using an HTTP proxy; WebSocket/gateway never establishes though curl via the same proxy succeeds. The Discord channel is thus completely non-functional despite correct proxy config in ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json.
summary.solution_summary: No fix provided in the thread. Reporter notes the proxy appears logged but not applied to the underlying Discord.js/fetch client; suggested workarounds are using alternative channels (Feishu) or a TUN-mode transparent proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: No maintainer response in the thread. The reporter supplied reproducible steps, logs, config, environment (OpenClaw 2026.3.13 on WSL2), and labeled severity as high.
summary.dedupe_summary: No duplicates referenced; issue appears unique and 100% reproducible when an HTTP proxy is required, likely needing a code change to ensure the proxy agent is passed to Discord.js/fetch clients.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that captures the core problem — Discord proxy is recognized but REST calls fail because the proxy agent isn't passed to Discord.js/fetch — and suggests the needed code change, with minimal noise.

results.title: Discord REST API ignores proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: Discord REST API calls from OpenClaw ignore the configured proxy and fail with "TypeError: fetch failed" when sending messages, while the WebSocket connection and curl via the same proxy work; Telegram and Discord WebSocket honor the proxy so the issue is specific to Discord REST requests.
summary.solution_summary: Make Discord REST requests use the same proxy-aware SSRF-guarded fetch path implemented for web_search/web_fetch so they honor channels.discord.proxy and HTTP(S)_PROXY environment variables.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: No maintainer response in the thread; reporter points to the 2026.2.26 changelog that fixed web_tools proxying but did not include Discord REST and requests that Discord REST be included in the fix; a webhook workaround was suggested.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related to the 2026.2.26 web_tools proxy fix but not a duplicate; should be tracked alongside web_fetch/web_search proxy issues and any other channel REST proxy reports.

judge.reasoning: The summary is meta (references a specific release and related fixes) rather than stating the core bug (Discord REST API ignoring proxy and fetch failures), so it lacks the concrete problem description needed for reliable deduplication.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to' routes to agent:*:main instead of live channel session

summary.problem_summary: In v2026.3.11 the CLI command 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to ...' routes deliveries to agent:*:main instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the session resolver does not search by delivery context, the command path omits channel/account context, Discord targets are not normalized, and the compact resolver lookup is incorrect.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch was applied to compiled dist files to add delivery-context-based session resolution, normalize Discord targets, pass channel and replyAccount/accountId through the command path, and fix the compact resolver; post-patch validation shows messages route to the correct channel/thread session.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is high-risk because the fix was applied to compiled dist files and will be lost on updates; maintainers should implement the fix in source, add tests, and include a post-update revalidation step. Affected compiled files and related issue numbers are listed in the report.
summary.dedupe_summary: Likely a regression related to existing reports about session routing fallbacks and Discord thread leakage (issues #36401, #41562, #16149); consider de-duplicating and tracking under a canonical regression issue.

judge.reasoning: The summary is too generic and meta—it only flags this as a regression and links other issues instead of describing the specific CLI session-resolver bug (delivery-context lookup, Discord target normalization), so it won't reliably cluster similar technical reports.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads - volatile metadata re-injection + CLI routing to wrong session

summary.problem_summary: Two regressions affecting Discord live sessions: volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history) was re-injected every turn causing context bloat and degradation, and CLI commands routed to the wrong session (defaulting to agent:*:main instead of the thread-specific session).
summary.solution_summary: Patched compiled runtime bundles to stop reinjecting thread metadata, added session resolution by actual delivery target and delivery-context lookup, propagated channel and replyAccount/accountId to the resolver, and corrected use of buildAgentMainSessionKey. Service was restarted and sessions verified to show correct toolCall outputs and no repeated context.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes were applied as manual edits to compiled bundles in openclaw/dist and verified by restarting openclaw-gateway.service; maintainers note this patch will be lost on update and recommend keeping a re-application script. Environment: OpenClaw 2026.3.11, Discord channel, systemd deployment.
summary.dedupe_summary: Treat as a single issue with two related root causes (runtime metadata injection and CLI resolver context); track by affected files (pi-embedded, gateway-cli, compact, register.agent) and symptoms (context bloat in threads, CLI commands landing in main). Mark as high priority and avoid reopening duplicates that describe the same Discord-thread metadata reinjection or CLI routing behavior.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that captures both core regressions (volatile metadata re-injection in Discord threads and CLI session resolver routing), affected components, and symptoms, with only a minor non-deduplication directive that isn't necessary for clustering.

results.title: [Bug]: tools/sandbox: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES (5 MB) is hardcoded and not user-configurable

summary.problem_summary: The sandbox media staging limit is hardcoded to 5 MB in dist/store-*.js (MEDIA_MAX_BYTES = 5 * 1024 * 1024), causing files larger than 5 MB to be rejected or silently fail in sandboxed agents. This regression exists in v2026.3.7 and v2026.3.8 and requires a manual post-install patch as a workaround.
summary.solution_summary: Expose the MEDIA_MAX_BYTES as a configurable option (e.g., tools.media.maxBytes in openclaw.json) or read the limit from existing per-type config, remove the hardcoded constant from built files, and apply a sensible default when unset so larger files can be allowed per user configuration.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Issue is labeled bug and regression and includes version, OS, repro steps and a workaround, but there are no maintainer comments or fixes in the thread yet.
summary.dedupe_summary: Likely unique but could overlap with other issues about media size limits or config not honored; check for prior issues mentioning MEDIA_MAX_BYTES or sandbox media size to avoid duplication.

judge.reasoning: The summary fails to state the core bug (sandbox MEDIA_MAX_BYTES hardcoded to 5MB and not user-configurable) and instead gives meta guidance about uniqueness, so it’s noisy and won’t help cluster similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord REST API requests fail with "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration being enabled

summary.problem_summary: Discord bot can receive messages via the Gateway but cannot send them: all REST API calls fail with 'fetch failed' despite proxy configured in openclaw.json, gateway logs showing 'rest proxy enabled', and curl requests using the same proxy succeeding. Environment: OpenClaw 2026.2.25 on macOS with Node.js 25.6.1.
summary.solution_summary: No resolution recorded in the thread. Suggested next actions are to verify OpenClaw actually applies the configured proxy to its REST client (and whether the underlying HTTP client supports proxies), try setting HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY environment variables, collect full stack traces and debug logs, and reproduce a failing REST call from Node to isolate whether undici/fetch is bypassing the proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Gateway logs indicate 'rest proxy enabled' and 'gateway proxy enabled' but also show repeated 'fetch failed' errors and failed native command deployment/fetching bot identity; no maintainer responses or fixes are included in the thread.
summary.dedupe_summary: No duplicates or related issues referenced in the thread; appears to be a new bug report specific to REST requests and proxy handling.

judge.reasoning: The summary is mostly metadata ('No duplicates...') and too generic—it fails to mention Discord, the 'fetch failed' error, or that REST requests via configured proxy are failing—so it won't reliably cluster similar issues.

results.title: Discord REST API (@buape/carbon) ignores channels.discord.proxy config

summary.problem_summary: @buape/carbon's RequestClient (and other globalThis.fetch call sites like memorySearch) do not honor channels.discord.proxy from openclaw.json, so REST API calls and some media downloads bypass the configured proxy while the WebSocket gateway correctly uses it. The root cause is RequestClient calling globalThis.fetch without a proxy-aware undici dispatcher and Node 22's native fetch not using env proxy vars by default.
summary.solution_summary: Change @buape/carbon's REST client to accept an optional dispatcher/proxy configuration and have OpenClaw pass channels.discord.proxy into it so REST calls use the same proxy as the gateway. Short-term workaround: preload undici's EnvHttpProxyAgent via NODE_OPTIONS to set a global dispatcher.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: No maintainer responses or actions recorded in the thread; the report includes a clear bug description, root cause analysis, a workaround, and an explicit suggested fix.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related to issue #25232 (attachment downloads bypass proxy) which shares the same root cause but affects a different code path; consider linking or consolidating fixes rather than duplicating efforts.

judge.reasoning: The summary only references a related issue and suggests consolidation without describing the actual technical problem (Discord REST API bypassing channels.discord.proxy), so it lacks the specific signal needed for reliable deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]: Performance degradation - context growth + lane blocking under load

summary.problem_summary: Under sustained load and long-running conversations OpenClaw experiences unbounded context token growth, increasing per-turn latency, Discord lane blocking/serialization under load, and typing indicators stuck until TTL in some paths.
summary.solution_summary: Applied configuration limits and timeouts: reduced concurrent session/agent/subagent limits, set agents.defaults.contextTokens to 80000, increased Discord eventQueue.listenerTimeout to 300000, improved patching script to cover hash variants, and restarted the gateway; post-restart no new lane-wait or typing-TTL errors observed.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Maintainers implemented and validated the config changes and hardening script, restarted the gateway, and confirmed the immediate errors stopped while noting restart is a mitigation not a full fix; they recommended making limits adaptive or exposing them as tunable config options.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related issues #27690 (lane blocking), #44026 (serialization by agentId), and #26733/#27172 (typing TTL) overlap with this root-cause; treat this thread as the umbrella/root fix and link or mark the related issues as duplicates or dependent.

judge.reasoning: The summary is an action note about linking related issue IDs rather than a concise description of the core problem (unbounded context growth, lane blocking, typing TTL under load), so it provides low useful signal for deduplication and won't reliably cluster similar technical reports.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread context re-injected every turn (context pollution + cache bust)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history) on every turn, causing context contamination, token bloat, prompt cache busting and degraded performance. The compiled runtime lacks a guard to limit this injection to the first turn, unlike prior Slack/iMessage fixes.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch to compiled dist files prevents repeated injection and adds an isNewSession guard: dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js stops injecting the listed metadata, and dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js adds the isNewSession check and replaces resolveAgentMainSessionKey with buildAgentMainSessionKey. Post-patch validation shows threads no longer repeat context and prompt caching is restored.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk because fixes were applied directly to compiled dist/ files and will be overwritten by updates; maintainers should apply the fix in source, include tests/guards, and add a post-update revalidation script. Issue is urgent and related to several open threads.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related issues appear to be duplicates or closely related (#41355, #34228, #37047) and should be consolidated under a single upstream fix for Discord thread metadata injection to avoid repeated reports.

judge.reasoning: Clearly identifies the core problem (Discord thread metadata injection) and intent to consolidate duplicates, but omits specific technical symptoms (which fields, cache/token impact) and includes issue-number noise.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread metadata re-injected every turn (context pollution)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 Discord thread messages repeatedly re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history) on every turn, causing context pollution, prompt cache misses, increased token usage and degraded performance in long threads. The compiled runtime in dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js treats Discord like other platforms but lacks guards limiting injection to the first turn.
summary.solution_summary: A local manual patch to dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js stops injecting the volatile Discord metadata after the first turn; post-patch validation shows eliminated repeated context, restored prompt caching, and reduced token usage. The change was applied directly to compiled files and verified, but not yet merged into source.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reporter applied a high-risk manual fix and requests an official source-level patch plus a post-update revalidation script because edits to dist/ will be overwritten by updates. No maintainer merge or official release yet; action recommended is to implement the fix in source and release a patched build.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related/overlapping issues: #41355 (ThreadStarterBody re-injected), #34228 (thread sessions reset), #37047 (starter metadata busting cache). This appears to be the same root cause as prior Slack/iMessage fixes and should be linked or deduplicated with those tickets.

judge.reasoning: The summary correctly links related issues and suggests a shared root cause but fails to state the specific bug (Discord thread metadata re-injection) or key affected file, so it has useful signal but is missing core details for reliable deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads root fix - runtime patches for context growth + session routing

summary.problem_summary: Two runtime regressions affecting Discord threads: per-turn re-injection of metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, InboundHistory) causing unbounded context growth and performance degradation, and the session resolver falling back to a global agent:*:main so CLI/session routing resolves to the wrong session.
summary.solution_summary: Manual patches were applied to compiled runtime bundles (several dist/*.js files) to fix context propagation and resolver lookup by delivery context, with an idempotent re-application script and timestamped backups created; gateway was restarted and verified, and a verification checklist completed.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: The fixes are manual edits to compiled bundles and will be lost on updates; maintainers should upstream the changes into source/builds, keep the re-apply script and backups, and add smoke/long-thread tests and monitoring to prevent regressions.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related to existing issues about context growth in long conversations, CLI session resolution, and Discord thread handling; treat this as the runtime/patch instance of those regressions and consolidate tracking under those prior reports.

judge.reasoning: The summary clearly identifies the core issues — context growth in long conversations, CLI/session resolution, and Discord thread handling as a runtime/patch instance to consolidate with prior reports — but omits some specific technical artifacts and field names present in the original which would be helpful for perfect matching.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord messages not being sent - "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw receives Discord messages and the model generates replies, but sending the final reply fails with "TypeError: fetch failed" despite logs showing both gateway and REST proxy enabled and the proxy working for curl. Occurs in DMs and group chats on OpenClaw 2026.3.2 running under Node 22.22.0.
summary.solution_summary: No fix or patch provided in the thread. Likely causes are the HTTP fetch call for sending replies not honoring the configured proxy or a runtime/fetch compatibility issue; recommended actions are to add fetch-stack debug logs, confirm proxy usage in the REST send path, reproduce with an equivalent curl request, and provide stack traces.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: No maintainer comments or triage activity in the thread; the issue was opened and labeled bug and regression but not acted on yet.
summary.dedupe_summary: No duplicate issues referenced; appears to be a unique report.

judge.reasoning: The summary does not describe the core technical problem (Discord proxy fetch failures when sending replies) and only states uniqueness, so it provides no useful signal for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI agent --channel discord --to routes to agent:*:main instead of live session

summary.problem_summary: In v2026.3.11 the CLI command openclaw agent --channel discord --to resolves to agent:*:main instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the compiled session resolver does not look up sessions by delivery context (channel+to+accountId) and Discord targets are not normalized (e.g. "148..." vs "channel:148...").
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch was applied to dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js to normalize Discord targets, resolve sessions by the real delivery context (channel+to+accountId), and lookup existing sessions before falling back to agent:*:main; post-patch validation shows messages route to the correct channel/thread session.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a high-priority regression that requires an upstream source-level fix and tests to persist across releases; the current hotfix is high-risk because it edits compiled files in dist and will be lost on reinstall/upgrade.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related to #41562 (Discord thread session delivery leaking to main session) and references #36401 (feature request for --session-key); likely a duplicate/should be tracked together with #41562.

judge.reasoning: The summary just marks the issue as related/duplicate and briefly mentions a Discord session leak without the key technical details (CLI flag, resolver behavior, file involved), so it's too generic and low-signal for reliable deduplication though it may still link to the referenced duplicate.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Regression in compact - resolveAgentMainSessionKey ReferenceError

summary.problem_summary: In v2026.3.11 the compiled file dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js calls a non-existent function resolveAgentMainSessionKey, causing ReferenceError at runtime; the agent command fails and gateways can crash. This regression was introduced by the prior session-resolver patch.
summary.solution_summary: Manually edited dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to replace resolveAgentMainSessionKey(...) with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...), which removes the ReferenceError and restores agent command and session behavior.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High priority and high risk: this is a regression in compiled/dist files that will be lost on update. Maintainters should fix the source, rebuild and publish a new release that includes this change, and ensure the fix is applied along with the earlier session-resolver fix.
summary.dedupe_summary: This is a follow-up regression to fix #3 (session resolver); fix #5 depends on fix #3 and both must be included in releases to avoid the ReferenceError.

judge.reasoning: The summary is vague and refers to internal issue numbers/dependencies rather than the concrete regression (incorrect use of resolveAgentMainSessionKey vs buildAgentMainSessionKey in the compiled compact bundle), so it offers poor, noisy signal for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord plugin fails with "fetch failed" when using proxy

summary.problem_summary: Discord plugin's channels.discord.proxy setting is applied only to WebSocket connections, not REST API calls, causing health checks and message sends to fail with "fetch failed" in proxy-required environments despite curl working via the same proxy.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the channels.discord.proxy value is used by the Discord REST HTTP client and healthcheck (route all HTTP requests through the configured proxy), add tests for proxied REST calls, and release a patch updating the plugin to use the proxy for both REST and WebSocket traffic.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Issue labeled bug and regression; no maintainer comments, assignment, or PR linked yet.
summary.dedupe_summary: Not marked as a duplicate and no related issues referenced; appears to be a new regression bug.

judge.reasoning: This summary contains only procedural/meta info and omits the core technical issue (Discord proxy setting not applied to REST API calls), so it would not help cluster similar bugs.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Session resolver in compact lacks delivery-context lookup

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 the compiled session resolver in dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js does not lookup sessions by delivery context (channel+to+accountId), so agent commands fall back to agent:*:main instead of resolving the live channel/session, causing routing and response association failures.
summary.solution_summary: A local manual patch to dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js adds delivery-context lookup (channel+to+accountId) in the agent session resolver and retains fallback to main; post-patch validation shows correct session resolution and consistent routing.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk: this is a compiled dist/ file change that will be overwritten by updates; maintainers should implement the fix in source/build, release an official patch, and prioritize preventing recurrence in future builds.
summary.dedupe_summary: Unique to v2026.3.11 and file dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js: session resolver missing delivery-context lookup; check for other reports in this release but treat as a single-source build artifact issue requiring source-level fix.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific about the core problem (session resolver in dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js missing delivery-context lookup), but includes minor triage/action noise (release-uniqueness and treat-as-build-artifact guidance) and an unnecessary version mention.

results.title: [Feature Request] Add i18n support for OpenClaw Control UI (Chinese localization)

summary.problem_summary: The Control UI has most interface text hardcoded in English, creating usability barriers for non-English speakers (notably Chinese) across settings, buttons, agent/skill pages, channel configuration, and status messages.
summary.solution_summary: Extract all hardcoded strings into an i18n layer (e.g., JSON locale files), add a zh-CN locale with translations, provide a language selector or auto-detect system language, and create infrastructure to add more languages later.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: No maintainer feedback shown in the thread; the reporter asks if this is on the roadmap and invites contributors, marking the request Medium-High priority for UX/accessibility.
summary.dedupe_summary: Project docs already include zh-CN translations and the control-ui bundle contains hardcoded strings; recommend checking for existing i18n issues or PRs to avoid duplicate work before proceeding.

judge.reasoning: Summary captures the key facts (docs have zh-CN, control-ui has hardcoded strings) but adds a minor coordination recommendation instead of succinctly stating the core request to add i18n support and locale files.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): add systemPrompt support for accounts and groups

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp channel could not apply channel-configured systemPrompt instructions at the account or group level, preventing per-account or per-group prompt customization and wildcard defaults.
summary.solution_summary: Adds systemPrompt support to WhatsApp config/schema with a resolution hierarchy (root account prompt overridden by account prompt; group prompt uses specific group then wildcard; account groups fully replace root groups). Implementation includes prompt composition (account prompt + resolved group prompt), tests, and docs updates.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Backwards compatible, WhatsApp-only change with no hot-reload/allowlist/mention-gating semantics altered; includes tests and docs and addresses issue #7011; PR is focused and will replace a previous broader PR.
summary.dedupe_summary: This PR supersedes the author's prior PR #40250 by providing a narrower change set; account-level groups replace root groups (no merge) and wildcard '*' semantics remain supported to avoid duplicate configs.

judge.reasoning: Summary emphasizes PR precedence and merge behavior (including a PR number) instead of the core feature—adding WhatsApp account/group systemPrompt support—so it contains noise and would not reliably cluster with similar feature changes.

results.title: Fix WhatsApp group append processing and account-scoped gatin

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group messages delivered via messages.upsert(type="append") were being dropped before agent processing, and group policy resolution could ignore account-scoped config so account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom fallbacks could fail, causing valid group messages to be rejected.
summary.solution_summary: Enable append processing only for WhatsApp group messages and propagate accountId when resolving WhatsApp group policy so account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom are considered; keep DM append history skipped and preserve existing inbound dedupe behavior.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Bugfix touching src/web/inbound/monitor.ts and src/web/auto-reply/monitor/group-activation.ts; no schema or dist changes, targeted tests added for account-scoped allowlist fallback, validated against live repro; backward compatible but watch for backlog/history processing risks and account-scoped behavior changes.
summary.dedupe_summary: Inbound dedupe remains in place and DM append history continues to be skipped to avoid replay; append processing is scoped to groups to prevent duplicate or unexpected processing of historical DMs.

judge.reasoning: Summmary correctly highlights preserving inbound dedupe and scoping append processing to groups, but omits the important account-scoped policy/gating change, so it may miss duplicates related to WhatsApp account-scoped policy resolution.

results.title: fix(gateway): resolve Canvas 401 behind Tailscale Serve

summary.problem_summary: Canvas/A2UI returned 401 when accessed via Tailscale Serve because resolveClientIp() returned undefined for requests from a trusted proxy that omitted forwarding headers, and isLocalDirectRequest() failed to fall back to the socket address when allowRealIpFallback was enabled and XFF resolved to a non-loopback Tailscale IP.
summary.solution_summary: resolveClientIp() now falls back to the socket remoteAddress when no forwarding headers are present; isLocalDirectRequest() checks req.socket.remoteAddress when allowRealIpFallback is enabled; isLocalishHost() gates .ts.net acceptance behind the existing allowTailscale flag and that flag is threaded through callers; docs updated and tests added.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All tests updated and passing; change is backward compatible and preserves existing behavior when tailscale.mode="serve" because allowTailscale defaults to true; users behind Tailscale Serve should set gateway.trustedProxies and gateway.allowRealIpFallback as documented; no new auth modes introduced and this is a security hardening fix.
summary.dedupe_summary: This closes issue #28472 and depends on PR #50636 for overlapping allowTailscale gating; no other duplicate issues noted.

judge.reasoning: The summary only references issue/PR numbers and dependency info and contains no technical description of the actual bugfix or changes, so it won't help cluster similar issues.

results.title: fix(security): block non-self messages in WhatsApp self-chat mode

summary.problem_summary: checkInboundAccessControl computed an isSelfChat flag but never used it as a guard, so when WhatsApp channel config lacked an explicit groupPolicy non-self messages (groups, stranger DMs, broadcasts) could pass through even in self-chat deployments, violating the intended isolation boundary.
summary.solution_summary: Add an early-return guard in checkInboundAccessControl that rejects any non-self message when account.selfChatMode === true, placed before group/DM policy evaluation; the change checks the explicit config flag (not the heuristic isSelfChat) and preserves isSelfChat in results for UI/logging.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All 9 existing access-control tests pass; author verified code paths and scenarios, limited the change to src/web/inbound/access-control.ts, documented intentional behavior (auto-detected self-chat unchanged), and provided quick-revert instructions; some review conversations remain for maintainer judgment.
summary.dedupe_summary: This PR is the canonical fix for explicit selfChatMode enforcement; it does not affect auto-detected self-chat heuristics or other channels/policies and may be followed by a separate change to handle heuristic-only cases if desired.

judge.reasoning: Concise and focused on enforcing explicit selfChatMode and excluding heuristics, but it omits the platform (WhatsApp) and the specific behavior change (an early-return guard that blocks non-self messages).

results.title: fix: store group messages from non-allowlisted senders as pending context

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy set to "allowlist", group messages from senders not in groupAllowFrom are dropped at access-control and never reach the mention-gating/context-storage logic, so agents cannot see non-allowlisted group messages as context even when docs describe storing them for context-only.
summary.solution_summary: Introduce an opt-in config flag groupContextFromAll (top-level and per-account, default false) that causes non-allowlisted group messages to be marked storeForContext/contextOnly and saved to pending group history (no replies or read receipts), plus related type/schema and pipeline plumbing changes.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: PR is opt-in and backward compatible by default, locally tested and small-scope, but Greptile flags a 3/5 confidence due to potential allowlist normalization mismatches and bypassing of group gating/state updates; labels include channel: whatsapp-web, stale, size: S.
summary.dedupe_summary: Likely overlaps with prior issues about group allowlist behavior, mention-gating, and context-storage; key de-dupe targets are any open issues on allowlist/normalization or 'store group messages for context' behavior, and this PR should be reconciled with discussions about applyGroupGating side effects.

judge.reasoning: The summary is vague and meta — it references related topics (allowlist, mention-gating, context-storage) but fails to state the core change (store non-allowlisted group messages as pending context) and includes noisy guidance about dedupe targets rather than a concise technical problem description.

results.title: feat(sessions_spawn): add sessionKey param to reuse sub-agent sessions

summary.problem_summary: sessions_spawn previously always created a new random-UUID session for each sub-agent spawn, preventing reuse of conversation history and allowing session keys to bypass allowlist/security checks.
summary.solution_summary: Introduce an optional sessionKey param: short keys map to agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}, fully-qualified keys are validated to match targetAgentId, and omission falls back to crypto.randomUUID(); add four tests for deterministic key, UUID fallback, fully-qualified passthrough, and cross-agent mismatch rejection.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reviewer flagged a blocking test compile error (importing non-existent openclaw-tools.js instead of clawdbot-tools.js), a case-sensitivity bug in fully-qualified key validation (embedded agentId not normalized), and inconsistent error handling (throw vs jsonResult); confidence low (2/5) and tests/files need fixes before merge.
summary.dedupe_summary: The test import looks like a typo causing a non-compiling duplicate test path; fix the import/name to align with existing clawdbot tests to remove redundancy and restore test coverage.

judge.reasoning: The summary describes a test import typo and duplicate test path unrelated to the PR's core change (adding a sessionKey for deterministic sub-agent sessions), so it would mislead clustering and fail to match similar items.

results.title: fix(telegram): auto-detect captionable messages for editMessageCaption

summary.problem_summary: Editing Telegram messages that contain media (photo, video, audio, document, animation) via the generic message(edit) flow called api.editMessageText(), causing Telegram to return 400 "there is no text in the message to edit" and leaving edits failing and UI inconsistent.
summary.solution_summary: editMessageTelegram now catches the specific 400 error (NO_TEXT_IN_MESSAGE_RE) and retries with editMessageCaption for captionable media; buttons-only updates call editMessageReplyMarkup directly; HTML parse-mode fallback and MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED handling preserved; six unit tests added to cover the paths.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Labels: channel: telegram, stale, size: M. Change is limited to src/telegram/send.ts, tests pass (510/510) with 6 new tests, Greptile confidence 5/5 and safe-to-merge signal from CI and test coverage.
summary.dedupe_summary: Backward-compatible fix with no interface or config changes and no linked/closing issue; risk noted that the error-regex may break if Telegram changes wording, but failures will gracefully propagate as before; revert by restoring src/telegram/send.ts if needed.

judge.reasoning: The summary focuses on backwards-compatibility and risk/revert notes but omits the core technical change (auto-detecting captionable messages and retrying with editMessageCaption on a specific 400), so it lacks the specific signal needed to cluster similar Telegram edit/caption bugs.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): restore config-driven block streaming for WhatsApp delivery

summary.problem_summary: PR #24962 hardcoded disableBlockStreaming: true for WhatsApp to block reasoning/thinking leaks, which unintentionally disabled block streaming entirely so messages were batched and only sent when the agent finished—even when per-account or global block streaming was configured.
summary.solution_summary: The deliver callback now only suppresses payloads with kind === "tool" while reasoning payloads are filtered upstream by shouldSuppressReasoningPayload, and disableBlockStreaming is now driven by per-account channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming with fallback to agents.defaults.blockStreamingDefault; tests and live verification show progressive delivery works and no reasoning/tool blocks leak.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All 13 inbound contract tests pass and live WhatsApp testing confirms progressive text_end streaming when enabled; default behavior remains off unless explicitly enabled. Quick revert options: set channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming:false or remove blockStreamingDefault; primary files touched include extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.ts and dispatch-from-config.ts. Original leak fixes (#24954, #24605) remain effective.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related: PR #24962 (introduced the regression) and issues #24954, #24605 (original leaks). Any open reports about WhatsApp streaming being disabled should be considered duplicates of this fix restoring config-driven block streaming.

judge.reasoning: Identifies that the PR fixes a WhatsApp block-streaming regression and links related reports, but omits the specific technical changes (restoring config-driven disableBlockStreaming and the deliver callback behavior) that would improve clustering accuracy.

results.title: fix(telegram): fix gateway hang on video messages and increase media download limit

summary.problem_summary: Sending videos (larger than the old 5MB default) caused the Telegram gateway to hang because content-length checks prevented response bodies from being consumed and Agent.close() deadlocked, rendering the channel unresponsive until restart.
summary.solution_summary: Raised default channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb from 5 to 20, fixed closeDispatcher deadlock by adding a timeout and fallback destroy, made media download failures non-fatal, added timeouts/retries for getFile and downloads, and updated stripInboundMetadata to recognize inbound media paths.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Labeled as a bugfix and security hardening affecting gateway, integrations, and UI; includes tests (strip-inbound-meta.test.ts 15/15) and live verification, is backward-compatible and configurable, introduces no new dependencies and preserves existing SSRF policy.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related to inbound media paths commit 49196e024; not a duplicate of SSRF or storage changes and no other duplicate PRs referenced.

judge.reasoning: The summary only references a commit hash and states it's not a duplicate of SSRF/storage changes, but fails to describe the core bug fixes or behavior (Telegram gateway hang, media download limits, timeout/retry fixes), so it provides almost no useful signal for clustering.

results.title: fix(telegram): use type-aware media placeholder for document attachments (#7116)

summary.problem_summary: Telegram inbound fallback body text used a hardcoded <media:image> for non-audio media when no caption/text was present, causing documents (PDF, DOCX) and other non-photo media to be misidentified and the agent to respond incorrectly.
summary.solution_summary: Change resolveTelegramInboundBody to use the already-computed type-aware placeholder (e.g. <media:document>, <media:video>) instead of hardcoded <media:image>, and add unit tests verifying placeholders, media detection, and getFile behavior for document messages.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: PR marked as a bug fix, closes #7116, limited scope (only agent-facing placeholder text), no security or permission changes, tests added and passing, reviewer conversations addressed with some unresolved items needing maintainer judgment.
summary.dedupe_summary: Root cause existed since original body computation (worked for photos but not other media); no duplicate fix noted in thread; new unit tests added to prevent regressions for document/media placeholder handling.

judge.reasoning: Captures root cause and that tests were added, but omits the key detail that the inbound fallback used a hardcoded <media:image> and was changed to a type-aware placeholder, and includes an irrelevant comment about the thread.

results.title: media: make MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable via media.maxBytes

summary.problem_summary: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES was hardcoded to 5 MB in src/media/store.ts so users could not raise the staging/upload limit, causing files larger than 5 MB (common PDFs, presentations, images) to fail to stage into sandboxed agents.
summary.solution_summary: Introduced a new configurable media.maxBytes (integer, optional, max 500 MB) read at gateway boot via get/set pattern, replaced hardcoded constant, updated error messages to reflect configured limit, preserved 5 MB default and kept deprecated constant for compatibility; unit tests pass.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: PR closes #40880, addressed all bot review threads, passed 56 unit tests, is backward compatible with no required migration, and documents risks (disk usage) and mitigations (500 MB schema cap, TTL cleanup).
summary.dedupe_summary: Change is scoped to inbound media staging only; per-channel download limits and tools.media.* limits are unchanged, so no overlap with those configs; default behavior preserved and the deprecated constant retained to avoid breaking external consumers.

judge.reasoning: The summary correctly states the scope and compatibility guarantees but omits the core actionable change (adding media.maxBytes and replacing the hardcoded MEDIA_MAX_BYTES), so it has useful signal but misses a key detail for accurate clustering.

results.title: fix: pass file metadata to agent when Telegram media download fails

summary.problem_summary: When Telegram media download fails (due to exceeding mediaMaxMb or Telegram API limits) the handler returned early and silently dropped the entire message, so the agent never saw the file or caption and the user received no ack or feedback.
summary.solution_summary: On download failure the code now extracts file metadata from the Telegram message, injects a metadata stub into msg.text (e.g. file name/size/type and reason), and lets the message continue through the normal pipeline so the agent can handle/respond; previous automatic user warning replies were removed.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is marked a bugfix for the Telegram integration (labels: channel:telegram, size:M), verified via code/trace analysis but not live runtime tested; change is backward-compatible, revertable by a single commit, and linked to issues #24854 and #23452; risk of mutating msg.text is acknowledged but limited to the prior-error path.
summary.dedupe_summary: Related to issues #24854 and #23452 addressing the same silent-drop behavior; no other duplicates called out in the thread.

judge.reasoning: The summary only references related issue numbers and 'silent-drop behavior' without describing the actual fix or symptoms, so it lacks the technical signal needed for deduplication.

results.title: feat(cli): add --session-key targeting for openclaw agent

summary.problem_summary: openclaw agent lacked a --session-key option, forcing callers to rely on rotating UUID sessionId, sender-derived --to, or --agent main sessions, which causes CLI-triggered deliveries to resolve to the wrong session in multi-channel/multi-agent setups and breaks callback/automation routing.
summary.solution_summary: Adds a new --session-key CLI flag, threads sessionKey through AgentCliOpts into resolveSessionKeyForRequest and gateway parameters, updates help/validation text, and keeps resolution precedence as session-key -> session-id -> to -> agent; includes tests verifying parsing and forwarding.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes are additive, backward-compatible, and well-tested (two unit tests passing locally). Implementation touched register.agent.ts and agent-via-gateway; reviewers indicated low risk and high confidence for merging.
summary.dedupe_summary: This PR addresses recurring session-targeting reports (issues #22085, #13635, #12881) by providing an explicit stable targeting path but does not claim to fully resolve those broader reports; behavior for existing callers remains unchanged.

judge.reasoning: Summary is vague and avoids the key technical change (adding --session-key to openclaw agent), instead referencing issue numbers and a generic “stable targeting path,” so it lacks the specific terms needed to cluster similar PRs.

results.title: feat: separate group-level allowlist from sender-level command authorization

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy set to "allowlist", inbound group interactions are currently gated by sender phone number (groupAllowFrom), which prevents allowing entire groups while letting any participant chat, restricts slash commands separately, and doesn’t stop the bot being added to spam groups.
summary.solution_summary: If groupPolicy is "allowlist" and a channels.whatsapp.groups mapping exists, access control now gates by group JID membership (allowing anyone in an approved group to chat) while keeping slash-command authorization governed by groupAllowFrom; when no groups mapping is present, the legacy sender-based filtering is preserved.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Automated Greptile review rates the change 4/5 and considers it safe to merge; core logic change is localized to access-control and tests were added for the new behavior, with one noted stale inline comment in the code/documentation to fix.
summary.dedupe_summary: No duplicate behavior introduced; change preserves backward compatibility and depends on PR #3326 to avoid implicitly enabling group allowlist when groupPolicy is not explicitly "allowlist".

judge.reasoning: Summary focuses on backward-compatibility and a PR dependency but omits the core change—separating group-level allowlist from sender-level command authorization—so it lacks the necessary signal to cluster related issues.

results.title: fix(telegram): propagate mediaMaxMb limit to outbound media reply delivery

summary.problem_summary: Configured telegramAccounts[].mediaMaxMb was not being applied to outbound reply messages with media because loadWebMedia in the reply delivery path always used the default 100 MB cap, so operators could not enforce lower limits for replies.
summary.solution_summary: Threaded mediaMaxBytes through the Telegram outbound reply delivery chain (bot.ts → bot-message.ts → bot-message-dispatch.ts → delivery.replies.ts) and the native-commands path, added unit tests to verify maxBytes is passed or omitted, and kept direct-send and internals unchanged.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Includes two new unit tests and reports 108 related tests passing across delivery, dispatch, native-commands, and bot-message suites; build and checks were verified, reviewer comments addressed, and the PR closes #46023.
summary.dedupe_summary: This is a minimal parameter-threading fix that preserves existing defaults and direct-send behavior, avoids changing media-loading internals, and also patches the parallel native-commands path to prevent duplicated bugs.

judge.reasoning: Summary correctly describes a parameter-threading fix and related paths but omits the specific Telegram/mediaMaxMb outbound-reply context and is a bit generic, so it may not reliably cluster with similar media-limit Telegram issues.

results.title: fix(telegram): add HTML parse fallback for media captions

summary.problem_summary: Media sends with captions lacked the HTML-parse fallback that text messages had, so captions with invalid HTML entities caused Telegram API 400 errors and the entire media send to fail.
summary.solution_summary: Wrapped the media send callback in withTelegramHtmlParseFallback so on an HTML parse error the send is retried without parse_mode using the raw caption; applies to photo, document, video, animation, audio, voice and video note sends and includes a unit test verifying the retry.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, backward-compatible bugfix touching src/telegram/send.ts; adds one unit test (total 52 passing). Labeled channel: telegram, size: S; quick revert by restoring the send.ts change.
summary.dedupe_summary: This reuses the existing text-message HTML fallback behavior (no duplicate logic) and only alters the media send inner callback; edit-message, thread fallback and caption splitting logic remain unchanged.

judge.reasoning: Concise statement that media sends now reuse the text-message HTML fallback and only modify the media-send callback (leaving edit/thread/caption-splitting logic alone), but it omits the explicit failure case and retry-without-parse_mode detail.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): make block streaming configurable and fix delivery race in block-reply pipeline

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp had block streaming effectively disabled (disableBlockStreaming hardcoded true) which batched intermediate text and introduced a delivery race: block replies were enqueued optimistically and the pipeline marked sentContentKeys before confirmed delivery, causing silent turns where neither blocks nor the final reply reached the user.
summary.solution_summary: Make block streaming configurable via channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming, fix the boolean activation bug, add blockStreamingCoalesceDefaults, implement dispatcher.sendBlockReplyAsync and await it in onBlockReply so sentContentKeys is set only after confirmed delivery, and update tests/mocks accordingly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Tests updated and passing and manual checks verify both streaming-on and off behaviors; the PR fixes the root cause (not just a workaround) and includes a usage command (openclaw config set ...) making it ready for merge.
summary.dedupe_summary: Previously dedupe suppressed final replies because the pipeline optimistically marked blocks as sent; now the pipeline waits for confirmed block delivery before marking sentContentKeys, and falls back to the final reply if block delivery fails, preventing silent turns.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that captures the core delivery-race bug and its fix (waiting for confirmed block delivery and falling back to final reply) without boilerplate, so it will cluster with similar fixes.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal channel lacked group-level allowlist support, so administrators couldn't explicitly allow specific Signal groups by ID to bypass sender-level groupAllowFrom checks or run control commands.
summary.solution_summary: Adds channels.signal.groups.<groupId> config with a SignalGroupConfig type and Zod schema, wires resolveChannelGroupPolicy into the Signal event handler to apply group-level bypasses for message delivery and command authorization, and updates docs; TypeScript compiles and manual testing was performed.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Introduces SignalGroupConfig (requireMention, tools, toolsBySender), integrates groupExplicitlyAllowed into commandGate authorizers, and uses hasGroupAllowFrom to maintain backward compatibility; follows patterns from other channels and is marked safe to merge by the reviewer.
summary.dedupe_summary: Supersedes PR #4337 and fixes #25540 by addressing Greptile review feedback (command gating and hasGroupAllowFrom handling); no other duplicate changes indicated.

judge.reasoning: Mentions fixing command gating and hasGroupAllowFrom and references PR/issue numbers, but omits the key 'Signal' and the new groups config detail and includes PR/issue boilerplate, so it has some signal but misses important specifics for reliable clustering.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal lacked group-level allowlisting via channels.signal.groups, so when groupPolicy was set to "allowlist" and groupAllowFrom contained group IDs, group messages were silently dropped because groupAllowFrom is a sender-level filter and never matches group IDs.
summary.solution_summary: Added a groups config to SignalAccountSchemaBase, invoked resolveChannelGroupPolicy in Signal's group access check before falling back to sender-level groupAllowFrom, and added four tests; all relevant test suites pass.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small-size PR labeled channel: signal and fixes #25540. Fully tested: 624 config tests, 6 Signal monitor tests, and 17 group-policy tests (including 4 new) all pass. PR was AI-assisted with human oversight by JamesPeck.
summary.dedupe_summary: This change brings Signal into parity with other channels that already support a groups config (Telegram, WhatsApp, iMessage, IRC, BlueBubbles); it fixes a unique missing call in Signal rather than duplicating an existing fix.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that Signal now supports a 'groups' config like other channels and fixes the missing group-level allowlist path, but it’s slightly vague about the exact schema/call added.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): support config-driven block streaming

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp messages are delivered as a single batch because process-message.ts hardcodes disableBlockStreaming:true, so intermediate blocks and tool-call/sleep-delimited messages are not sent separately; there was also a bug treating queuedFinal as the only delivered marker.
summary.solution_summary: Respect channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming by adding resolveWhatsAppBlockStreamingEnabled and replacing the hardcoded flag, fix queuedFinal logic so intermediate block payloads count as replies, register blockStreaming capability and default in the WhatsApp dock, and update tests to cover enabled/disabled behavior.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Tests were updated and all 10 existing tests pass; tests now mock counts like other channels and verify anyReplyDelivered for intermediate blocks. Change is backward-compatible by default (blockStreaming false) and labeled channel: whatsapp-web, size: S.
summary.dedupe_summary: This change aligns WhatsApp with existing Telegram/Line blockStreaming behavior to avoid duplicate feature-requests; if blockStreaming is omitted it remains backward-compatible, so duplicate issues should reference this PR and the shared blockStreaming config.

judge.reasoning: Summarizes the core change (WhatsApp adopting Telegram/Line blockStreaming behavior and remaining backward-compatible) but includes meta/process noise about duplicate feature-requests and omits the important technical fix around queuedFinal/intermediate payload handling.
